EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of November 3rd, scattered showers along with isolated thunderstorms, developed and moved into the central and eastern portions of the greater Phoenix area from the southeast. The showers produced pockets of gusty east winds which reached upwards of 45 mph at times; the gusty winds were sufficient to uproot a tree in the Scottsdale area at about 0700MST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and isolated thunderstorms developed and moved into the central portion of the greater Phoenix area from the southeast during the early morning hours on November 3rd. The showers generated strong and gusty outflow winds which spread north into portions of Scottsdale. According to local broadcast media, at 0700MST an easterly gust to 45 mph uprooted a small tree near 48th Street and Indian School Drive about 3 miles west of Scottsdale.

CZ_NAME: UPPER GILA RIVER AND ARAVAIPA VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong trough of low pressure moved across Southeast Arizona the night of January 31 into February 1. This produced very strong winds across Southeast Arizona, especially in the mountains with Mt. Lemmon gusting over 100 MPH. Valley locations across Southeast Arizona generally saw wind gusts of 35 to 55 MPH with a few spots especially near Sierra Vista with gusts over 60 MPH. Snow was also observed in elevations primarily above 4000 feet with the mountains seeing brief blizzard-like conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Guthrie RAWS recorded a wind gust to 75 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The wettest and coldest in a series of Pacific storms systems moved into Arizona on January 7th and produced heavy mountain snow. Snow amounts across much of the higher terrain of Southeast Arizona above 7000 feet was in excess of 1 foot. Valley rainfall amounts averaged from 0.50 to 1.25 inches in the Tucson area, which caused some minor flooding of low-lying areas and road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of generally between 0.50 and 1.25 inches during the afternoon of January 7 caused multiple road closures across Pima County, including parts of the Tucson metro. One car stalled near the intersection of Lee St. and Santa Rosa Ave in approximately 6 inches of water during the rush hour. About 12 hours later, 2 people became stranded in their vehicle after it became stuck in a running wash on Limberlost Road just west of Soldier Trail just before dawn on January 8. No injuries or fatalities occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong trough of low pressure moved across Southeast Arizona the night of January 31 into February 1. This produced very strong winds across Southeast Arizona, especially in the mountains with Mt. Lemmon gusting over 100 MPH. Valley locations across Southeast Arizona generally saw wind gusts of 35 to 55 MPH with a few spots especially near Sierra Vista with gusts over 60 MPH. Snow was also observed in elevations primarily above 4000 feet with the mountains seeing brief blizzard-like conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind gusts occurred especially in the Sierra Vista area with a measured gust to 65 MPH near Hereford. There were also numerous gusts of 58 MPH or higher including the Canelo area and Pioneer airfield. These strong wind gusts resulted in seven downed power poles near Sierra Vista along Highway 92 and closed the highway for several hours. One house had a portion of its roof torn off by the wind.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The higher elevations of Graham and Greenlee County received 6 to 12 inches of snow combined with wind gusts in excess of 50 MPH, which caused brief blizzard-like conditions. This event began on January 31.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong trough of low pressure moved across Southeast Arizona the night of January 31 into February 1. This produced very strong winds across Southeast Arizona, especially in the mountains with Mt. Lemmon gusting over 100 MPH. Valley locations across Southeast Arizona generally saw wind gusts of 35 to 55 MPH with a few spots especially near Sierra Vista with gusts over 60 MPH. Snow was also observed in elevations primarily above 4000 feet with the mountains seeing brief blizzard-like conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 75 MPH was recorded with 5 inches of snow on Mt. Hopkins observatory. Power lines were also knocked down near Bisbee. This event began on January 31.

This produced very strong winds across Southeast Arizona, especially in the mountains with Mt. Lemmon gusting over 100 MPH. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably cold Pacific storm system combined with unseasonably high moisture levels to produce a rare widespread late April rain event over the Mojave Desert.  One report of flash flooding was received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One foot of water flowed over Bank Street at Gordon Drive.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The St Johns Airport ASOS measured a peak wind gust of 62 MPH at 438 PM.  The winds gusted over 55 MPH between around 300 PM MST and 530 PM. An observer 8 miles southwest of Concho reported a peak wind gust of 58 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitable water at or near all-time April records and an unseasonably strong, cold Pacific storm system came together to produce a very unusual April flash flooding episode.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water about 1.5 feet deep flowed over roads in White Hills after 0.49 inch of rain fell in 20 minutes. Washes were also out of their banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Approaching low pressure, which was situated across the Great Basin, caused gusty west to southwest winds across the state. A poorly maintained field was source of blowing dust which caused a multi-vehicle accident and closed Interstate 10 in Cochise County for two hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Southwest winds gusting up to 45 mph blew dust from an non-maintained parcel of land adjacent to Interstate 10 across the roadway causing near-zero visibility during the afternoon hours. The result was a collision involving two tractor-trailers heading westbound near Mile Post 376, about 15 miles west of the New Mexico/Arizona state line. Both vehicles and their payloads sustained substantial damage. Sixty miles of interstate were closed for about 2 hours due to both blowing dust to the collision.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure residing northwest of the area resulted in another day of gusty southwest winds due and blowing dust across Interstate 10 in Cochise County. A preemptive closure of Interstate 10 for several hours prevented any accidents from occurring.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Interstate 10 was closed for 2 periods during the afternoon due to blowing dust causing near-zero visibility. The first closure was for approximately 1 hour, from 3 to 4 pm, between Mile Post 352 and 378. The second closure was once again from Lordsburg, NM to Willcox, AZ from approximately 5 pm through 8 pm. No accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure creating a tight pressure gradient across southeast Arizona was located near the northwest corner of the state. This resulted in gusty west to southwest winds, which produced blowing dust, several collisions and a subsequent closure of Interstate 10 in Cochise County. Wind damage also occurred in Douglas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blowing dust once again originating from an non-maintained parcel of land adjacent to Interstate 10 caused near-zero visibility during the early afternoon hours. This resulted in 3 collisions involving a total of 12 vehicles within a mile of Mile Post 376. The vehicles involved included a San Simon fire truck as well as a DPS SUV. No serious injuries were reported, as the DPS officer was 50 ft away from his vehicle when it was struck by a semi, and the fire chief had also exited his vehicle when impact occurred. The interstate was closed, from Lordsburg, NM to Willcox, AZ from 2 pm through 7 pm in both directions for a total of 60 miles. Traffic was rerouted along State Route 191/70 through Safford, AZ, a detour of approximately 110 miles. Winds were from the southwest with gusts to between 35 and 40 mph at the time of the collisions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure creating a tight pressure gradient across southeast Arizona was located near the northwest corner of the state. This resulted in gusty west to southwest winds, which produced blowing dust, several collisions and a subsequent closure of Interstate 10 in Cochise County. Wind damage also occurred in Douglas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong southwest winds of 45 to 55 mph damaged the roofs of several apartments in the 800 block of Eighth Street between C and D Avenues in Douglas. The units were determined unsafe to live in until repairs could be made.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure off the southern California coast resulted in gusty southwest winds over southeast Arizona. The wind caused blowing dust from a recently cleared parcel of land adjacent to Interstate 10 in Cochise County to blow across the roadway causing near-zero visibility, a traffic accident and subsequent road closure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Southwest winds gusting up to 30 mph blew dust from an non-maintained parcel of land adjacent to Interstate 10 across the roadway causing near-zero visibility during the afternoon hours. The result was a two vehicle accident near Mile Post 376, about 15 miles west of the New Mexico/Arizona state line. Sixty miles of interstate were closed for about 2 hours due to both blowing dust to the collision.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dust devil cause damage to a house and property.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A dust devil damaged a dog run and the roof of a house. The large dog run was thrown approximately 60 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Bert Fire was started by lighting on May 29 near Highway 180 about 10 miles southeast of Valle. This natural fire was allowed to burn in a fire adapted ecosystem. The fire reached about 200 acres by the end of May and a total of 6000 acres by June 27.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bert Fire was started by lighting on May 29 near Highway 180 about 10 miles southeast of Valle. This natural fire was allowed to burn in a fire adapted ecosystem. The fire reached about 200 acres by the end of May and a total of 6000 acres by June 27.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A spring storm system brought snow to portions of northern Arizona. Some areas  received snow advisory amounts or less. A few areas received heavy snow amounts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tsaile received 8.2 inches of snow (1.02 liquid) overnight and into the morning hours.  A trained spotter 8 miles ENE of Chinle reported 5.5 inches of snow at 6400 feet.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Juniper Fire started about 10 miles south of Young on May 20th in ponderosa pine, mixed conifer, and oak shrub. By the end of May, the fire burned about 7,800 acres.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Jack Fire started with a lightning strike near Happy Jack about 50 miles southeast of Flagstaff. This fire was allowed to burn as a natural part of this ecosystem within a predetermined area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning started the Jack Fire near Happy Jack in a ponderosa forest on May 29 around 115 PM. This ecosystem is fire adapted and the fire was allowed to burn within a set boundary. The fire burned about 500 acres in May and reached a final size of 33,850 acres by June 24 when progression stopped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A spring snow storm brought snow to northern Arizona above elevations of around 6000 feet. Areas between around 6000 and 7500 feet got advisory amounts of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten inches of snow fell at Hart Prairie at an elevation of 8400 feet. Snow began to fall around 2 PM on April 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced hail in Yarnell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported in Yarnell with brief heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm dropped heavy rain over the Juniper Fire scar. This caused flash flooding and debris flows over Highway 288.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced between 0.50 and 1.25 inches of rain over the Juniper Fire scar. Debris flows closed Highway 288 at MP 277 at Pocket Creek. Reynolds Creek washed out the highway at MP 286. There was a minor washout on Cherry Creek Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A gust front from a thunderstorm interacted with another thunderstorm updraft to create a vortex that caused property damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A confirmed gustnado was on the ground for approximately 4 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Tenderfoot fire was human caused and burned around 4,100 acres just northeast of Yarnell in chaparral and grass. .
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tenderfoot fire was human caused and started about a mile south Yarnell. Strong southwest winds pushed the fire to the northeast and east of Yarnell in chaparral and grass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The lightning caused Wildcat Fire burned around 1,500 acres on the east side of the Kaibab Plateau (which contains the North Rim of the Grand Canyon) between June 13 and June 22.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The lightning caused Wildcat Fire burned around 1,500 acres on the eastern slopes of the Kaibab Plateau. Strong southwest winds pushed the fire northeastward and away from Highway 67. This highway is the only paved road that provides access to the North Rim of the Grand Canyon. This fire caused the closure of several trails in the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A record high of 87 degrees was set at the Flagstaff airport. The previous record was 86 set for this date was 86 set in 1996.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flood debris reached the Arizona Department of Transportation yard near  Workman Creek. The flow included large boulders, deep mud, and debris from recent fire activity.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and dime sized (0.70 inch diameter) caused flowing water in area washes/road crossings.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An inch and a half of rain caused Big Bug Creek to flow about 2 feet from the top of the bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture crept into southeast Arizona, resulting in thunderstorms across Santa Cruz, Greenlee and southern Cochise counties. One storm became severe in Santa Cruz County.

The Fuller Fire started with a lightning bolt near Fuller Canyon on the North Rim of the Grand Canyon near Cape Royal Road. Fire is a natural part of the ecosystem on the Kiabab Plateau so this fire was managed for resource benefit. The fire grew to 9 acres by July 10. Then southwest winds pushed the fire northeast with active spotting. Southwest winds continued to increase through July 15 (4.5 mile run) with the greatest|spread on the 16th as the fire burned out large bowls and side canyons filling in unburned areas. The fire was 14,385 acres by July 23. By August 14th, the fire had slowly reached 14,541 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fuller Fire started with a lightning bolt near Fuller Canyon on the North Rim of the Grand Canyon near Cape Royal Road. Fire is a natural part of the ecosystem on the Kiabab Plateau so this fire was managed for resource benefit. The fire grew to 9 acres by July 10.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Cedar Fire started southwest of Show Low on June 15th. The fire burned north-northeast, and east for three days for a total of about 13,000 acres. Then, on June 19th, strong northeast winds caused the fire to jump containment lines and burn 14,000 acres in one day.  This also was one of the hottest days on record with highs 10-15 degrees above average for the day. The total size by June 27 was about 46,000 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cedar Fire started southwest of Show Low on June 15th. The fire burned north, northeast, and east for three days for a total of about 13,000 acres. Then, on June 19th, strong northeast winds caused the fire to jump containment lines and burn 14,000 acres in one day. This also was one of the hottest days on record with highs 10-15 degrees above average for the day. The total size by June 27 was about 46,000 acres.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over the central Arizona deserts shortly after noon on June 11th, and due to elevated levels of moisture and instability, they were able to generate strong and gusty winds which affected portions of northern Pinal County. Winds in excess of 60 mph were measured at the Casa Grande airport shortly after 1300MST although no damage was reported. Large hail was also reported near the airport at about the same time. The strong thunderstorms led to the issuance of a variety of products such as Severe Thunderstorm Warnings and Significant Weather Advisories.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over the central deserts during the early afternoon hours on June 11th and they affected the community of Casa Grande. One of the stronger storms produced large hail which fell a few miles southeast of the Casa Grande airport. According to a trained weather spotter, at 1300MST large hail estimated to be about 0.88 inches in diameter fell about 2 miles north of Casa Grande, or nearly 3 miles southeast of the Casa Grande airport. No damage was reported as a result of the large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong high pressure developed in the upper atmosphere across the desert southwest during the middle of June, beginning on Saturday, June 18th and continuing into the middle of the following week. The very warm temperatures aloft, combined with dry air and sunny skies allowed high temperatures to climb to dangerous levels across the lower deserts each day. Desert high temperatures on Saturday reached or exceeded 110 degrees in many locations and by Sunday, many hotter deserts ranged between 115 and 120 degrees. An Excessive Heat Warning was issued for all of the lower Arizona deserts, starting 1000MST Sunday and continuing through the middle of the following week. The dangerous heat led to the deaths of multiple people, including a mountain biker in the greater Phoenix area during the morning hours on Sunday, June 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Unusually strong high pressure aloft developed across Arizona during the middle of June, beginning Saturday June 18th and persisting into next week. The strong high pressure, combined with dry air and sunny skies, led to very dangerous heat across the greater Phoenix area on Sunday June 19th. An Excessive Heat Warning was in effect starting at 1000MST and continuing through the evening hours; the high temperature in Phoenix reached 118 degrees which set a new record for the date. This dangerous heat led to the death of a mountain biker in the Phoenix Mountain Preserve during the morning hours. According to spokesman Captain Larry Subervi of the Phoenix Fire Department, fire crews responded to reports of an adult with heat related issues about 1 mile from the Desert Vista Trailhead shortly before 0900MST. This was in the area near Interstate 17 and Jomax Road. The 28 year old woman, a personal trainer, had set out biking on the trail with two friends at about 0600MST and they all had water. Shortly before 0900MST, her friends - who are doctors - started CPR and called 911. She soon stopped breathing. Crews reached the woman about 0930MST and continued to provide aid while airlifting her to the parking lot where an ambulance awaited. According to Captain Subervi, she was transported to a local emergency room in extremely critical condition and she lost her pulse due to the exhaustion. He indicated that there were no other injuries and her condition was 100 percent heat-related illness. The woman, who was an experience biker, became tired in the heat and told her friends that she could not continue before gaining an altered mental status. Tom Grace, a technician on the helicopter that airlifted her off the trail, said that the operation was one of the riskiest types of rescues performed by the department due to the heat and the location of the woman.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over the central Arizona deserts shortly after noon on June 11th, and due to elevated levels of moisture and instability, they were able to generate strong and gusty winds which affected portions of northern Pinal County. Winds in excess of 60 mph were measured at the Casa Grande airport shortly after 1300MST although no damage was reported. Large hail was also reported near the airport at about the same time. The strong thunderstorms led to the issuance of a variety of products such as Severe Thunderstorm Warnings and Significant Weather Advisories.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across portions of the central deserts during the early afternoon hours on June 11th and some of them moved through the community of Casa Grande. One of the stronger storms produced strong and gusty winds at the Casa Grande airport. At 1315MST, the official AWOS weather station at the Casa Grande airport measured a wind gust to 61 mph. No damage was reported at the airport due to the strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well-developed, southeasterly monsoonal circulation had ushered in ample amounts of moisture to south central Arizona and combined with an unstable air mass, this allowed for isolated to scattered thunderstorms to form over portions of the south central deserts on June 29th. Some of the late afternoon and evening storms brought strong, gusty and damaging winds to northern Pinal County, and these winds affected communities such as Magma. Wind gusts, measured in excess of 70 mph, blew down trees as well as power poles. Injuries were not reported as a result of the strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over portions of northern Pinal County during the late afternoon hours on June 29th and some of them affected the areas around the town of Magma. Some of the stronger storms produced strong gusty winds. An SRP weather station near the intersection of East Bella Vista Road and Quail Run Lane, about 2 miles northeast of Magma, measured a wind gust to 71 mph at 1800MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well-developed, southeasterly monsoonal circulation had ushered in ample amounts of moisture to south central Arizona and combined with an unstable air mass, this allowed for isolated to scattered thunderstorms to form over portions of the south central deserts on June 29th. Some of the late afternoon and evening storms brought strong, gusty and damaging winds to northern Pinal County, and these winds affected communities such as Magma. Wind gusts, measured in excess of 70 mph, blew down trees as well as power poles. Injuries were not reported as a result of the strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense thunderstorms developed across portions of south-central Arizona during the late afternoon hours on June 29th and one of them affected the area around the community of Magma.  The severe thunderstorm produced gusty outflow winds estimated to be at least 70 mph in strength, and according to local broadcast media, at 1800MST the winds blew off a large portion of the roof of a block wall house. The house was located about 4 miles to the southeast of the town of Magma. No injuries were reported due to the strong winds. A Severe Thunderstorm Warning was in effect at the time of the damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well-developed, southeasterly monsoonal circulation had ushered in ample amounts of moisture to south central Arizona and combined with an unstable air mass, this allowed for isolated to scattered thunderstorms to form over portions of the south central deserts on June 29th. Some of the late afternoon and evening storms brought strong, gusty and damaging winds to northern Pinal County, and these winds affected communities such as Magma. Wind gusts, measured in excess of 70 mph, blew down trees as well as power poles. Injuries were not reported as a result of the strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across portions of northern Pinal County during the late afternoon hours on June 29th and they moved through the community of Magma. Some of the stronger storms generated gusty and damaging winds estimated to be in excess of 70 mph. According to local law enforcement, at 1750MST gusty outflow winds blew down 100 feet of power poles as well as power lines in the town of Magma. A Severe Thunderstorm Warning was in effect for the town of Magma at the time of the wind damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well-developed, southeasterly monsoonal circulation had ushered in ample amounts of moisture to south central Arizona and combined with an unstable air mass, this allowed for isolated to scattered thunderstorms to form over portions of the south central deserts on June 29th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon hours over portions of northern Pinal County on June 29th and they affected the area around the community of Magma. Some of the stronger storms produced gusty and damaging outflow winds estimated to be in excess of 60 mph. According to local broadcast media, at 1800MST gusty winds knocked over a parked tractor trailer loaded with large hay bales. Three large awnings covering the hay bales were destroyed. This occurred about 5 miles east of Magma, near the intersection of North Yeager Road and East Arizona Farms Road. No injuries were reported. A Severe Thunderstorm Warning was in effect for the town of Magma during the time of the wind damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong high pressure developed in the upper atmosphere across the desert southwest during the middle of June, beginning on Saturday, June 18th and continuing into the middle of the following week. The very warm temperatures aloft, combined with dry air and sunny skies allowed high temperatures to climb to dangerous levels across the lower deserts each day. Desert high temperatures on Saturday reached or exceeded 110 degrees in many locations and by Sunday, many hotter deserts ranged between 115 and 120 degrees. An Excessive Heat Warning was issued for all of the lower Arizona deserts, starting 1000MST Sunday and continuing through the middle of the following week. The dangerous heat led to the deaths of multiple people, including a mountain biker in the greater Phoenix area during the morning hours on Sunday, June 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very strong high pressure aloft developed across Arizona during the middle of June 2016, beginning on Saturday June 18th and persisting into early next week. In combination with dry air and sunny skies, the strong high pressure led to the development of dangerous heat across the lower central deserts. The high temperature for the day was 111 at Phoenix and although an Excessive Heat Warning was not yet in effect, heat impact level data suggested that borderline warning conditions had already developed across the greater Phoenix area and northern Pinal county. According to the Pinal County Sheriff's Office, a 25 year old man died while hiking on the Peralta Trail in Gold Canyon during the afternoon hours on June 18th. The victim died as a result of exposure to extreme heat; search and rescue teams were called out to the trail just after 1330MST. According to the Sheriff's Office, a friend told deputies that the victim started hiking at 0730MST for what was to be a day hike; unfortunately he ran out of water after just a few hours. Shortly thereafter he exhibited signs of heat illness and the friend called 911 for help. The man's condition continued to get worse, he lost consciousness and never recovered. This was just one of three heat-related emergency calls within a four hour period on Saturday in the Superstition wilderness. The other two victims ended up with just minor heat-related injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first monsoon moisture push of the season led to isolated thunderstorms on the 27th through the 29th, followed by a widespread severe weather and flash flooding outbreak on the 30th. The episode continued into July 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds produced dust storm conditions on Stockton Hill Road, and then gusted to 60 mph at the Kingman Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture moved northern over northern Arizona and provided the fuel for strong thunderstorms to develop with hail, strong winds, and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Radar estimated that 0.5 to 1.2 inches of rain fell over the Cedar Fire scar. A 12 to 18 inch crest moved down Cedar Creek and through the town of Cedar Creek. This is the first of many post fire floods that happened during the summer of 2016.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense high pressure over the southwest U.S. led to record-breaking temperatures of 108 to 114 degrees in the lower elevations of southeast Arizona. No heat-related fatalities occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very hot temperatures over a four-day stretch contributed to at least 14 serious heat-related illnesses in Eastern Pima County alone.  A 72-year old man was airlifted to the hospital after he suffered heat exhaustion on Tanque Verde Falls hiking trail on June 3rd. Tucson International Airport tied a record high of 107 degrees on June 3rd, and set new records of 110 on June 5th and 111 on June 6th, respectively.  Organ Pipe National Monument set new record highs of 111 on June 3rd and 114 on June 5th. Temperatures during the period were as hot as 108 in Oracle and Sasabe, 109 in the Sierra Vista/Benson area and 112 in Sells.

A 72-year old man was airlifted to the hospital after he suffered heat exhaustion on Tanque Verde Falls hiking trail on June 3rd. Tucson International Airport tied a record high of 107 degrees on June 3rd, and set new records of 110 on June 5th and 111 on June 6th, respectively.  Organ Pipe National Monument set new record highs of 111 on June 3rd and 114 on June 5th. Temperatures during the period were as hot as 108 in Oracle and Sasabe, 109 in the Sierra Vista/Benson area and 112 in Sells.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense high pressure over the southwest U.S. led to record-breaking high temperatures of 108 to 119 degrees and resulted in four fatalities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A three-day stretch of very hot temperatures resulted in four heat-related fatalities and at least 19 heat-related illnesses in southeast Arizona, all of which were reported in Eastern Pima County. Out of three men who were hiking the Ventana Canyon Trail Sunday, June 19th, two died of heat stroke, although one was not found until Tuesday. The third man suffered heat-related injuries but survived. A woman died of heat stoke that same day hiking Finger Rock Trail. Her companion was airlifted to a hospital for treatment of heat-related illness. Also on that same day, a woman walking the city's Loop walking and biking trail succumbed to heat stroke near Interstate 10 and Irvington. Tucson International Airport recorded its third hottest day ever and the warmest day since 1994 with a record setting 115 degrees on June 19th. Other record highs on the 19th included 115 degrees at Picacho Peak, 119 at Organ Pipe National Monument, 112 at Safford and 109 at Nogales and Bisbee-Douglas Airport. On June 20th, additional record highs were set although the actual temperatures were not quite as hot.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With an inverted trough located over Sonora, Mexico, and a daily record 1.46 precipitable water values, thunderstorms formed over southeast Arizona, some of which were severe with large hail and damaging winds in the Tucson and Safford areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Facebook reports from the public indicated a measured 64 mph wind gust at Safford Ranch. One large tree was uprooted and fell on a vehicle. The Safford Electric Division reported power lines snapped and 3 or 4 poles had to be replaced. A chain linked fence was blown over.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports and photos of trees uprooted and broken branches on the northwest side of Tucson. Some branches fell on vehicles. Wind damage to a church roof led to extensive water damage inside.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued abundant moisture led to another round of severe thunderstorms in Tucson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds damaged a fence and snapped 4 tree branches on the northwest side of Tucson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed over Tucson.  No wind damage was reported, but a home was damaged after being struck by lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A home on the 1700 block of S. Avenida Regulo was damaged when a lightning strike caused a fire. The residents were not home at the time and no was was injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely separated thunderstorms occurred across southeast Arizona.  The strongest caused a swath of significant tree damage, heavy rain and flash flooding in Tucson's midtown area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports from just east of the University of Arizona campus to near River Road and Campbell of large trees uprooted, limbs snapped, and power lines down. The roof of Covenant Generations church on Tucson Blvd was severely damaged. Trees fell on carports and homes in the Winterhaven area of town. Severe damage was sustained at the Campbell Ranch Apartments as trees fell on buildings and vehicles. Major tree damage occurred at Himmel Park. For some residents, power was not restored until noon on June 27th. One person was electrocuted after coming in contact with a live downed wire but survived.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: City of Tucson reported a lightning strike to a home near 22nd St and Kolb Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely separated thunderstorms occurred across southeast Arizona.  The strongest caused a swath of significant tree damage, heavy rain and flash flooding in Tucson's midtown area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple swift water rescues within the midtown area. A family of 4 became stranded near Lee St. and 9th Ave when their vehicle stalled in flood waters up to their windshield. Other rescues were needed when vehicles traveling through roads covered in water became inoperable. Rainfall amounts of 0.50 inches in 15 minutes occurred, with storm total amounts of up to 2 inches across parts of the metro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep tropical moisture spread northward and across the central deserts of Arizona during the morning hours on September 7th; the moisture was remnants of dissipating Tropical Storm Newton. The moisture resulted in widespread moderate rainfall across much of southwest Maricopa County which began in the early morning and continued well into the day. Embedded thunderstorms caused locally heavier rainfall amounts that led to episodes of urban and small stream flooding to the southwest of the greater Phoenix area. Flash flooding did not occur, but rainfall was heavy enough to warrant the closure of Highway 238 near Mobile and to the east of Gila Bend during the middle of the morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Considerable amounts of tropical moisture associated with former Tropical Storm Newton moved into the central deserts during the morning hours on September 7th. The moisture led to widespread light to moderate showers along with embedded thunderstorms and this resulted in small stream flooding to the southwest of the greater Phoenix metropolitan area. According to the Department of Highways, flooding forced the closure of Highway 238 east of Gila Bend and about 4 miles northeast of the town of Mobile. The closure, which affected the area between mileposts 24 and 44, started at 0930MST. No flash flooding was observed in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Considerable amounts of tropical moisture associated with former Tropical Storm Newton spread across the southwest and south-central Arizona deserts during the morning hours on September 7th. The moisture led to widespread light to moderate showers along with embedded thunderstorms. The thunderstorms enhanced rainfall totals which resulted in episodes of small stream flooding across the southwest deserts. Small Stream Flood Advisories were issued during the morning hours, and the rainfall was sufficient to force the closure of Palomas Road about 9 miles to the northeast of the town of Mohawk. Flash flooding did not occur.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Considerable tropical moisture, associated with former Tropical Storm Newton, spread north and into the southwest deserts during the morning hours on September 7th. The moisture led to widespread light to moderate rain along with embedded thunderstorms. The storms enhanced rainfall totals and this resulted in some small stream flooding across portions of  Yuma county, mainly north of Interstate 8. According to Yuma County Emergency Management, at 1030MST Palomas Road became flooded at Milepost 55. The location of the flooding was about 9 miles northeast of the town of Mohawk. Flash flooding was not observed and there were no reports of accidents or injuries due to the flooded roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: It does not take dangerous or excessive levels of heat during the summer in southern Arizona to cause serious, even fatal, repercussions to the populace of Phoenix. Friday September 9th was a normal summer day in September; the high in Phoenix was 103 degrees and that is just 1 degree above the seasonal normal for the date. Despite the near normal nature of the heat, a man visiting from Switzerland went hiking in the afternoon and he was overcome by the heat, eventually passing away. Fire crews performed CPR on him to no avail. Triple digit heat can be very dangerous, especially to someone who is not used to the heat and who does not take the proper precautions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near normal summer heat occurred in the greater Phoenix metropolitan area on September 9th, the high in Phoenix was 103 compared to the normal of 102. Despite the near-normal nature of the heat, a man from Switzerland who was not used to triple digit heat died while hiking Camelback Mountain in Phoenix during the afternoon. The story was reported on the ABC15 website. According to the Phoenix Fire Department, the man had been hiking Camelback Mountain in the afternoon with 3 friends before he became separated from the group. He was heard yelling for help by employees of the Phoenician Resort who then went to meet him. The man was nearly on resort property when he began behaving erratically and then stopped breathing. Fire crews soon arrived and began performing CPR while on the way to the Phoenix hospital; at the hospital he was pronounced dead.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved east across southeast Arizona. Some storms were severe and produced large hail and damaging winds in Tucson and Oracle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to 1 inch in diameter occurred for a time near along Kolb Road near Escalante Rd, 29th St, and Golf Links Rd. One home near Kolb Rd and 29th St sustained damage when hail broke 5 double-paned skylights.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved east across southeast Arizona. Some storms were severe and produced large hail and damaging winds in Tucson and Oracle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golfball size hail was reported in Oracle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved east across southeast Arizona. Some storms were severe and produced large hail and damaging winds in Tucson and Oracle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree fell on a mobile home near Flowing Wells Rd and Wetmore Road causing damage. The tree was dead and had little root system. An 81-yr old woman and her cat were rescued by fire fighters but were uninjured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure residing northwest of the area resulted in another day of gusty southwest winds that caused blowing dust and the closure of Interstate 10 in Cochise County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blowing dust limited visibilities to near zero along Interstate 10 near Milepost 376 for much of the afternoon. This prompted the closing of the interstate from Lordsburg, NM to Willcox, AZ from approximately 2 pm through 6 pm. No accidents occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved east across southeast Arizona. Some storms were severe and produced large hail and damaging winds in Tucson and Oracle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds uprooted a tree near Golf Links Rd and Prudence Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing monsoon moisture brought thunderstorms with heavy rain over Yavapai County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Granite Creek rose 3.5 feet near Prescott with a peak flow of 1468 CFS. An upstream gauge rose 4 feet with a peak flow of 1209 CFS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing monsoon moisture brought thunderstorms with heavy rain over Yavapai County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced 1.42 inches of rain over about 2 hours. Minor street flooding and flowing washes were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over Texas and a low pressure center off the California Coast brought increasing moisture to northern Arizona. Thunderstorms over far north-central Arizona and southern Utah produced flash flooding in the very narrow Paria Canyon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused the Paria River at Lees Ferry to rise 4 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure across northeastern Arizona and low pressure over southwestern Arizona created a tight pressure gradient primarily across central Arizona. Breezy to windy conditions were observed across the Mogollon Rim and points south as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The RAWS site 7 SSE of Crown King reported a gust of 62 mph at 4:48 AM MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure approaching the area from the northwest resulted in another day of gusty southwest winds and blowing dust across Interstate 10 in Cochise County. A preemptive closure of Interstate 10 for several hours prevented any accidents from occurring.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blowing dust limited visibilities to near zero along Interstate 10 near Milepost 376 from late morning into early evening. The interstate was preemptively closed from Lordsburg, NM to Willcox, AZ from approximately 10 am through 7 pm. Thus, no accidents occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved northeast across Pinal and Graham counties during the afternoon hours. One storm became severe producing large hail near Oracle Junction.
A tight pressure gradient due to low pressure located over central Baja California caused gusty easterly winds and localized wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained easterly winds at 25 to 35 mph with gusts up to 50 mph caused multiple power poles to be bent down along Madera Canyon Road. At least one will need to be replaced.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved east or northeast across southeast Arizona. One storm produced large hail in Sierra Vista.

A weak low centered over Baja California gradually moved northward across Arizona during the day. Moisture over far northern Mexico moved northward during the day and combined with upward motion to create strong to severe thunderstorms over the western part of the White Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported one half inch to one inch diameter hail covering the ground between 2 and 8 miles north of Show Low.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across portions of the greater Phoenix metropolitan area during the late afternoon hours on September 1st and the stronger storms were able to produce locally heavy rainfall sufficient to produce areas of street flooding. Some of the heavier rains occurred in the southeast portion of the greater Phoenix area including communities such as Chandler, Tempe and Ahwatukee. Peak rainfall rates were measured to be as high as 2 inches per hour, sufficient to overwhelm drainage systems and produce street flooding. An Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisory was issued for the greater Phoenix area as a result of the heavy rains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across the southeast portions of the greater Phoenix metropolitan area during the afternoon hours on September 1st and some of the storms produced locally heavy rain with peak rainfall rates as high as 2 inches per hour. The heavy rains led to areas of urban street flooding in communities such as Ahwatukee, Chandler and Guadalupe. At 1705MST a trained weather spotter located 3 miles south of Guadalupe estimated that one half inch of rain fell within 22 minutes. The heavy rains flooded the parking lot at Sprouts, which was located at the intersection of Interstate 10 and Ray Road. An Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisory was issued for the area beginning at 1731MST and it remained in effect through 1850MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passing through the Mojave Desert brought a band of thunderstorms well ahead of it, and strong winds with dust storm conditions immediately ahead of it.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads in Colorado City were closed due to flooding and debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A funnel clouds was seen near Williams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was seen about two miles south-southwest of Williams.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure centered off the southern California coastline caused another day of gusty southwest winds, which created blowing dust and the closure of Interstate 10 in Cochise County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blowing dust limited visibilities to near zero along Interstate 10 near Milepost 376 late in the day. This prompted the closing of the interstate from Lordsburg, NM to Willcox, AZ from approximately 6 pm through 8 pm. No accidents occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure centered over southern Nevada caused another day of gusty southwest winds, which created blowing dust and the closure of Interstate 10 in Cochise County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blowing dust limited visibilities to near zero along Interstate 10 near Milepost 376 for much of the day. This prompted the closing of the interstate from Lordsburg, NM to Willcox, AZ from approximately 2 pm through 6 pm. No accidents occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure residing across the Great Basin resulted in another day of gusty southwest winds that caused blowing dust and the closure of Interstate 10 in Cochise County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blowing dust limited visibilities to near zero along Interstate 10 near Milepost 376 for much of the afternoon. This prompted the closing of the interstate from Lordsburg, NM to Willcox, AZ from approximately 1 pm through 4 pm. No accidents occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved northeast across Cochise County during the afternoon. One storm produce heavy rain that caused localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced brief heavy rain that caused 6 to 12 inches of water to flow over Dragoon Road and Old Ranch Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Newton barely weakened to a remnant low before crossing into Arizona. It brought strong winds and decent rainfall totals to much of southeast Arizona.  In spite of rain amounts of 3 to 6 inches in the Santa Rita, Santa Catalina, Rincon and Pinaleno Mountains, the 24 hour duration of the rain event precluded much in the way of flooding from occurring. The Sierra Vista area was most adversely affected by flash flooding on September 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A swift water rescue occurred on South Burro Drive south of Ramsey Road in Hereford. Two people and their dog were rescued when their vehicle was washed down a low water crossing. No injuries occurred. Additionally, a portion of Leonardo de Vinci Drive in Sierra Vista was closed due to flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep monsoonal moisture continued to move northward over Arizona thunderstorms and heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain in Utah caused the Paria River to flash flood as it flowed south into Arizona and into the Colorado River. The Paria River was estimated to rise more than 2 feet at the Utah Arizona border in around 15 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep monsoon moisture continued to move northward over Arizona with scattered heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hassayampa River rose about 8 feet and was flowing rapidly. It is normally dry.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep monsoon moisture continued to move northward over Arizona with scattered heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Big Bug Creek rose an estimated 4 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture remained over northern Arizona with showers and thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 1.20 inches of rain in one hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture remained over northern Arizona with showers and thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported along Highway 191 at mile post 389.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture remained over northern Arizona with showers and thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported over Highway 191 near mile post 389.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture remained over northern Arizona with showers and thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 2.10 inches of rainfall in 45 minutes in a thunderstorm. Three and a half inch branches were also blown down in the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a few days of drying, moisture surged back over Northern Arizona with more showers and thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar sized (1.25 inch diameter) hail fell at the Rim Club Golf Course near Payson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 70 knot jet streak moved over the area during the early morning hours and triggered thunderstorms with heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over the Cedar Fire scar caused flash flooding in Cedar Creek. The water over topped the K-rails that were put in place after the fire to prevent flood damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture continued to move northward over Arizona fueled strong thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mesonet station in Minnehana (WAGA3) recorded 2.05 inches of rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture continued to move northward over Arizona fueled strong thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Stanton Mesonet Station (ZSTA3) measured 1.93 inches of rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture continued to move northward over Arizona fueled strong thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Towers Mountain Mesonet station MNNA3 recorded 1.89 inches of rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 70 knot jet streak moved over the area during the early morning hours and triggered thunderstorms with heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over the Cedar Fire scar caused flash flooding and debris flow over Highway 60. The water pushed large boulders over Highway 60.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 70 knot jet streak moved over the area during the early morning hours and triggered thunderstorms with heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms caused flash flooding which deposited large logs and rocks across area roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture began to decrease over Arizona during the day. However, there was enough moisture for a few storms to produce heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A flood wave hit State Highway 288 that washed debris over the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture began to decrease over Arizona during the day. However, there was enough moisture for a few storms to produce heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused mud and debris (8 to 12 inches deep) to flow over a few roads in Yarnell. A fence around a home was destroyed by the mud and debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture began to increase over northern Arizona isolated thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A photo of a broken 4 inch diameter tree branch was at the Cameron Trading post was posted on-line.  Storm lasted from 530 PM to 7 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture began to move back over northern Arizona with isolated thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced 1.50 inches of rain in 60 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well-defined upper low over northern Arizona brought scattered strong morning thunderstorms to northeastern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cold core funnel cloud was seen as far away as Gallup, New Mexico.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture continued to move northward over Arizona fueled strong thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mid Martinez Creek Mesonet station (ZMMA3) recorded 1.10 inches of rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture continued to move northward over Arizona fueled strong thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mesonet gauge in Yarnell recorded 2.17 inches of rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture continued to move northward over Arizona fueled strong thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mesonet Station 4 miles south of Yarnell (QSTA3) recorded 1.80 inches of rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture continued to move northward over Arizona fueled strong thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mesonet station 14 miles east-southeast of East Fork (QLMA3) recorded 2.16 inches of rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture continued to move northward over Arizona fueled strong thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mesonet station CPA3 (22 miles north of Yava) measured 1.95 inches of rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture continued to move northward over Arizona fueled strong thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Chambers Mesonet station (PUCA3) recorded 1.62 inches of rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep monsoonal moisture over Arizona brought thunderstorms and locally heavy rain across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The USGS stream gauge at Cherry Creek measured a 6 foot rise with a peak stage of 8.07 feet (4,800 CFS). Base flow is less than 10 CFS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture continued to move northward over Arizona fueled strong thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mesonet station 2 miles north-northeast of Kachina Village (FLAA3) recorded 1.33 inches of rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture continued to move northward over Arizona fueled strong thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mesonet station 7 miles south-southeast of Crown King (QHBA3) recorded 1.42 inches of rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture remained over northern Arizona with showers and thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Arizona Department of Transportation reported water on Highway 160 between Mile Post 432 and 445. Debris were also reported on the road near Mexican Water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture remained over Arizona with scattered thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch diameter hail fell for the second time in the Doney Park area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture remained over Arizona with scattered thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch diameter hail fell in Doney Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep monsoon moisture returned to northern Arizona with more strong thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The river gauge on Oak Creek in Sedona (Tlaquepaque) showed a 3 foot rise starting around 215 PM MST and peaked around 230 PM MST. The thunderstorm was moving from north to south through Oak Creek Canyon and then out over the Sedona area. Smaller washes then started flowing and caused the closure of several roads (Back 'O Beyond,  Sunset Drive, and Doodlebug Drive).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy rainfall and flash flooding occurred from Santa Cruz and southwest Cochise counties northwest across eastern Pima and southern Pinal counties. Tropical Storm Javier caused the marked increase in moisture across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of about 2.5 inches in 1 hour caused flash flooding south of Sierra Vista. Miller Creek overflowed Moson Rd while Ramsey Rd and Arabian Rd were also flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy rainfall and flash flooding occurred from Santa Cruz and southwest Cochise counties northwest across eastern Pima and southern Pinal counties. Tropical Storm Javier caused the marked increase in moisture across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to four inches or more of rainfall in one to two hours caused Sonoita Creek to overflow its banks into parts of Patagonia. Eight mobile homes were evacuated as a precaution with water 18 inches deep surrounding them. An additional 14 homes were also evacuated. Only minimal damage occurred to the skirting of these homes since they were elevated off the surface.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy rainfall and flash flooding occurred from Santa Cruz and southwest Cochise counties northwest across eastern Pima and southern Pinal counties. Tropical Storm Javier caused the marked increase in moisture across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to four inches of rainfall in 1 to 2 upstream, Sonoita Creek overtopped the spillway at Patagonia Lake and flooded areas below the Lake downstream to Rio Rico. A portion of Pendelton Drive near Avenida Coatimundi in Rio Rico where Sonoita Creek crosses the road was barricaded during the day. At 2115 LST a motorist was stranded after he drove around the barricades and attempted to cross the water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy rainfall and flash flooding occurred from Santa Cruz and southwest Cochise counties northwest across eastern Pima and southern Pinal counties. Tropical Storm Javier caused the marked increase in moisture across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of 1.5 to 2.5 inches in 90 minutes caused widespread flash flooding in Tucson and resulted in a total of 45 stranded motorist calls and 8 swift water rescues. One rescue involved a Pima County Supervisor, who was swept away in her vehicle near Ft. Lowell and Oracle Rd. Flood waters caused major road damage near Lee St. and Santa Rosa Avenue. The Stone St. underpass was flooded to a depth of 8 feet. Flash flooding also caused delays and closures along Sandario Rd in Picture Rocks. Near Silverbell Rd and Speedway Rd, multiple lower level townhouses incurred flood damage. A few residences in Green Valley suffered minor flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy rainfall and flash flooding occurred from Santa Cruz and southwest Cochise counties northwest across eastern Pima and southern Pinal counties. Tropical Storm Javier caused the marked increase in moisture across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain upstream from Tucson and along smaller washes near Ironwood National Monument caused strong flows in the Santa Cruz River, which breached a dirt barrier near Sunland Gin Road south of Arizona City. The water reached four feet at its maximum depth. The roadway was closed for nearly two days which cutoff the only access for residents of Silverbell Estates to the southwest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture associated with Javier continued to produce widespread heavy rainfall. Flash flooding was limited to southeast Pinal County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused washes to overtop secondary roads between Kearny and Florence. A |32-yr old man attempted to drive his truck across a wash near Whitlow Ranch and Hawkview Roads and became stuck when water rushed into his vehicle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Leftover moisture from Tropical Storm Javier continued to cause heavy rain and flooding on August 11th, mainly in Pinal County. Runoff continued into August 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused ponding of water along Highway 79 north of Park Link Drive, and caused Durham Wash and other small washes to flow across the highway, making it impassable for 2 hours. Farther south, heavy rain also occurred near I-10 along the Pinal/Pima county line. Flash flooding occurred at Pinal Air Park, where flood waters entered several buildings. Runoff flooded small washes near Red Rock and washed out Sasco Road at the Santa Cruz River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms damaged a silo near Safford. Flooding caused road closures and evacuations to continue in Pinal County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty thunderstorm outflow winds destroyed a metal silo. The winds moved the nearly empty silo about 60 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few thunderstorms developed across southeast Arizona from Tucson northward. One thunderstorm produced damaging winds in Oro Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving thunderstorm moved through Oro Valley, causing extensive wind damage. The strongest winds snapped three power poles  along Hardy Rd near La Canada. A tree 12 inches in diameter was broken in half at the base on Magee Rd between La Cholla Rd and La Canada Rd and widespread tree damage occurred near CDO High School, the Oro Valley Aquatic Center and at numerous residences. Around 2000 TEP customers were without power for portions of the night and into the early morning hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep monsoonal moisture over Arizona brought thunderstorms and locally heavy rain across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Radar estimated up to 1.60 inches of rain over the Turkey Track drainage. A 3 to 4 foot rise was observed downstream on Cedar Creek at the Highway 73 bridge in the community of Cedar Creek. Time is an estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms were widespread across southeast Arizona. Some storms produced heavy rain which caused flash flooding in Nogales, and rural sections of Pima and Pinal counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding across Park Link Drive over one foot deep in some spots. This caused some of the edges of the road to be washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture continued to move northward over Arizona fueled strong thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two feet of water moved through a restaurant, the Yarnell Post Office was inundated with water, and portions of Highway 89 was close due to debris and mud.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture continued to move northward over Arizona fueled strong thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 9.1 foot rise was reported on Martinez Creek. Residents downstream reported washed out roads.  Some area were not accessible because of the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture continued to move northward over Arizona fueled strong thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An 8 foot rise in water was reported at the bridge in Cedar Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture continued to move northward over Arizona fueled strong thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 288 was impassable at mile post 277 due to downed logs that washed onto the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Leftover moisture from Tropical Storm Javier continued to cause heavy rain and flooding on August 11th, mainly in Pinal County. Runoff continued into August 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several inches of rainfall caused significant flows on the McClellan and Durham Washes. Water traveled downstream, overflowing at in spots into parts of Picacho and Eloy. This caused flooding and a few evacuations of homes east of I-10. Significantly impacted areas included Picacho Blvd at Oak Lane, Picacho Hwy at Camino Adelante, and Houser Rd at SR-87.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture continued to move northward over Arizona fueled strong thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 2-3 foot surge in water prompted a swift water rescue involving 5 people and one pet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture continued to move northward over Arizona fueled strong thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Washes were running across portions of State Route 97. High clearance vehicles could make it across the washes without any issues while smaller cars had to turn around. Rainfall total was estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture continued to move northward over Arizona fueled strong thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports from the public were received of a few inches of water flowing over Highway 89. Both location and amounts were estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms were widespread across southeast Arizona. Some storms produced heavy rain which caused flash flooding in Nogales, and rural sections of Pima and Pinal counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nearly two inches of rain in parts of Nogales, Sonora caused Nogales Wash to overtop the Doe St. bridge in Nogales, Arizona.  As a result, businesses were flooded along Hohokam Drive with surface runoff 8 inches deep. Morley Avenue was also flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy rainfall and flash flooding occurred from Santa Cruz and southwest Cochise counties northwest across eastern Pima and southern Pinal counties. Tropical Storm Javier caused the marked increase in moisture across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused Peck Canyon to overflow its banks and flood adjacent streets in Rio Rico.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep monsoon moisture continued to move northward over Arizona with scattered heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observed rise in the Paria River gauge at Highway 89 (in Utah) was around 745 PM MST on August 4th. The crest reached the Lees Ferry gauge at around 415 AM on August 5th. Flash Flood levels in excess of 5 feet were estimated to have been in the Arizona section of the Paria River from 11 PM on August 4th through 7 AM on August 5th before entering the Colorado River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Yet more monsoon moisture, this time aided by an approaching upper level low and jet streak, brought a mainly nocturnal outbreak of severe weather and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was substantial damage near and southwest of the airport, including a 40 foot tall tree which landed on a mobile home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep monsoonal moisture continued to move northward over Arizona thunderstorms and heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A debris flow was reported across Highway 191 at mile marker 289 to 390. The road was temporarily close.  Radar estimated an inch of rain in less than 60 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms were widespread across southeast Arizona.  Slow-moving storms produced heavy rain and caused flash flooding, particularly along the perimeter of the Tucson metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding and 3 swift water rescues on the periphery of the Tucson metro area. The first rescue occurred around 830 am LST on Silverbell Rd between Ina Rd and El Camino del Cerro. Around the same time, flood waters 1 to 2 feet deep rushed across Sahuarita Rd near Calle Rinconado. A swift water rescue by the Corona de Tucson FD occurred around 10 am on South Houghton Rd near East Andrada Rd. A third rescue of a woman and her child occurred around 1120 am on South Desert Sunrise Trail near W Snyder Hill Rd. Both Andrada and Pantano High Schools were evacuated and closed early as a precaution due to road closures and a possible sinkhole. Classes resumed the next day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms were widespread across southeast Arizona.  Slow-moving storms produced heavy rain and caused flash flooding, particularly along the perimeter of the Tucson metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two homes on Camino del Rancho were flooded, displacing 9 people. The Red Cross provided temporary housing the the families. Numerous roads were closed around town. Over 1 inch of rain fell, and culverts were damaged along SR-80. Effects were exacerbated from heavy rain which also fell the previous day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms were widespread across southeast Arizona. Some storms produced heavy rain which caused flash flooding in Nogales, and rural sections of Pima and Pinal counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused Blanco Wash to flood Tucker Road west of Picture Rocks. Several inches of water also covered the road near Avra Valley Rd and Anway Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely separated thunderstorms moved slowly across southeast Arizona. One storm produced severe wind gusts in central Cochise County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter estimates thunderstorm wind gust of 60 mph north of Sunsites.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture remained over Arizona which lead to the development of thunderstorms. Some storms produced heavy rain and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 1 1/4 inch hail in Prescott.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture remained over Arizona which lead to the development of thunderstorms. Some storms produced heavy rain and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported in Gisela with washes running high.  A few road crossings were temporarily impassible because of the water and deposited debris on the roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture remained over Arizona which lead to the development of thunderstorms. Some storms produced heavy rain and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 3/4 to one inch diameter hail covering the ground in Mormon Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture remained over Arizona which lead to the development of thunderstorms. Some storms produced heavy rain and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Martinez Wash rose 5.7 feet in less than an hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Enough monsoon moisture remained over northern Arizona for isolated thunderstorms with heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Meteor Crater Visitor Center reported that at least 4 inches of water was running across Meteor Crater Road in places. Radar estimated that several inches of rain fell across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Enough monsoon moisture remained over northern Arizona for isolated thunderstorms with heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flowing water across low water crossings in the Dewey area. This included the Agua Fria River, Highway 69, and other local roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain in the greater Flagstaff area and south of Page.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS employee reported 1.46 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain in the greater Flagstaff area and south of Page.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Flagstaff Airport reported 1.25 inches of rain in 57 minutes and a total of 1.33 inches of rain in 90 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain in the greater Flagstaff area and south of Page.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A video showed that the Tanner Wash was flowing under the Highway 89 bridge. Rainfall amount was estimated from radar to be around 2.00 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced isolated heavy rain and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to three feet of water covered roads that crossed Black Canyon Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Fuller Fire started with a lightning bolt near Fuller Canyon on the North Rim of the Grand Canyon near Cape Royal Road. Fire is a natural part of the ecosystem on the Kiabab Plateau so this fire was managed for resource benefit. The fire grew to 9 acres by July 10. Then southwest winds pushed the fire northeast with active spotting. Southwest winds continued to increase through July 15 (4.5 mile run) with the greatest|spread on the 16th as the fire burned out large bowls and side canyons filling in unburned areas. The fire was 14,385 acres by July 23. By August 14th, the fire had slowly reached 14,541 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fuller Fire started with a lightning bolt near Fuller Canyon on the North Rim of the Grand Canyon near Cape Royal Road. Fire is a natural part of the ecosystem on the Kiabab Plateau so this fire was managed for resource benefit. The fire grew to 9 acres by July 10. Then southwest winds pushed the fire northeast with active spotting. Southwest winds continued to increase through July 15 (4.5 mile run) with the greatest spread on the 16th as the fire burned out large bowls and side canyons filling in unburned areas. The fire was 14,385 acres by July 23. By August 14th, the fire had slowly reached 14,541 acres.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep monsoonal moisture continued to move northward over Arizona thunderstorms and heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused street flooding in Flagstaff. Some homes and businesses had water up to the door steps. Flooding occurred near the major intersection of Milton and Butler/Route 66 , West Dupont Avenue, and Blackbird Roost Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep monsoon moisture continued to move northward over Arizona with scattered heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Prescott Valley reported 1.19 inches of rain in 35 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe weather and flash flooding which began on June 27th with the first monsoon moisture push of the season continued through July 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were washed out and basketball size boulders dislodged in and around Oatman.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe weather and flash flooding which began on June 27th with the first monsoon moisture push of the season continued through July 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nine power poles were blown down in Bullhead City and Mohave Valley. Several trees were blown down in Mohave Valley, and there was minor damage to structures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe weather and flash flooding which began on June 27th with the first monsoon moisture push of the season continued through July 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rocks and debris washed onto AZ Highway 95.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe weather and flash flooding which began on June 27th with the first monsoon moisture push of the season continued through July 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hackberry Road was closed from Truxton Wash to I-40 due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe weather and flash flooding which began on June 27th with the first monsoon moisture push of the season continued through July 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Signal Road was closed from Highway 93 to Alamo Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced severe winds and heavy rain over Yavapai County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Humbug Creek RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 61 MPH in association with a thunderstorm in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced severe winds and heavy rain over Yavapai County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Stanton RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 58 MPH with a thunderstorm in the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fuller Fire started with a lightning bolt near Fuller Canyon on the North Rim of the Grand Canyon near Cape Royal Road. Fire is a natural part of the ecosystem on the Kiabab Plateau so this fire was managed for resource benefit. The fire grew to 9 acres by July 10. Then southwest winds pushed the fire northeast with active spotting. Southwest winds continued to increase through July 15 (4.5 mile run) with the greatest spread on the 16th as the fire burned out large bowls and side canyons filling in unburned areas.
